title: Why Computing Moved Into the Cloud

lesson.learn:
Cloud computing lets people rent computing power, storage, and software over the internet instead of buying and maintaining everything locally. That makes digital tools easier to scale up or down, which is why small apps and giant services can both use the same basic model.

The real breakthrough is flexibility. Companies no longer need to own every server they use, and users can reach powerful services from almost anywhere as long as they have a network connection.
lesson.simpler: The cloud is like renting computers over the internet. You use what you need without having to own all the hardware yourself.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Cloud computing turns computing power into an on-demand utility.

learnImageCredit.creator: Derrick Coetzee from Berkeley, CA, USA

lesson.deeperDive:
Cloud systems rely on virtualization, distributed storage, orchestration, and redundancy. A single service might be spread across many machines so it stays fast and resilient even when traffic spikes or hardware fails. That design also makes upgrades easier, because providers can move workloads behind the scenes without users noticing.

The cloud is not magic and it is not literally in the sky. It is a network of physical data centers, cables, servers, and software layers that behave like one flexible platform.
lesson.funFact: The "cloud" is just a lot of very real servers.
references: Cloudflare Learning Center - cloud computing basics

people: J. C. R. Licklider - early networked-computing thinker
John McCarthy - imagined computing as a utility
Jeff Bezos - helped scale public cloud infrastructure
Werner Vogels - influential cloud architecture leader
places: Data centers - the physical home of cloud services
Google data centers - large-scale service operations
Fiber backbone networks - high-speed cloud connectivity
years: 2020s - hybrid and edge cloud become normal
concepts: Virtualization - one machine acting like many
Elasticity - scaling resources up or down quickly
Distributed computing - work spread across many machines
Multi-tenancy - serving many customers from one system
Pay-as-you-go - paying only for what you use
